name: Al Green
description: During the early 1970s, Al Green achieved massive commercial success recording a string of hit singles and albums for Hi Records in Memphis. His sound was defined by smooth, falsetto-laden vocals and a sophisticated blend of Southern soul and R&B produced by Willie Mitchell. This classic period established him as one of the most gifted purveyors of soul music before he transitioned to gospel later in the decade.

key_albums_70s.title: Al Green Gets Next to You

key_songs_70s: I'm Still in Love with You
trivia: In 1974, a domestic incident involving scalding grits led Green to deepen his religious faith and eventually become an ordained pastor.
His signature song 'Let's Stay Together' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1972.
He self-produced the 1977 project 'The Belle Album', which marked his final major secular success of the era.

name: Al Jarreau
description: Al Jarreau established his solo career in the mid-1970s with a distinctive blend of jazz-fusion and R&B. After being spotted by Warner Bros. Records in 1975, he released the critically acclaimed debut album We Got By. His early work showcased his signature scat singing and vocal percussion styles. He earned his first Grammy Award in 1978 for the album Look to the Rainbow.
trivia: He was spotted by Warner Bros. Records in 1975 while working with pianist Tom Canning.
On Valentine's Day 1976, he performed on the 13th episode of NBC's Saturday Night Live.
He won the 1978 Grammy Award for Best Jazz Vocal Performance for Look to the Rainbow.

name: Al Wilson
description: Al Wilson was a prominent soul singer known for his smooth vocal delivery and crossing over into the adult contemporary market. His 1970s output was characterized by romantic ballads and polished R&B production. He remains a significant figure in the evolution of soulful pop during the decade.
key_songs_70s: I Won't Last a Day Without You
I've Got a Feeling (We'll Be Seeing Each Other Again)
trivia: His 1973 single 'Show and Tell' was a million-selling number one hit on the Billboard Hot 100.
Wilson worked as a mail carrier and janitor in California before his musical career took off.
He developed a stand-up comedy routine as a backup plan in case he did not succeed as a singer.

name: Ann Peebles
description: Ann Peebles was a powerful presence in 1970s Southern soul, recording most of her classic material for Hi Records. Her sound was gritty yet polished, often dealing with themes of heartbreak and resilience. She is recognized for bringing a distinctive female perspective to the Memphis soul scene dominated by male artists.

key_albums_70s.title: The Handwriting Is on the Wall

trivia: Her 1973 hit 'I Can't Stand the Rain' was later described by John Lennon as one of the best songs ever.
Peebles was a key artist for the Memphis-based Hi Records, working alongside producer Willie Mitchell.
She began her professional career singing with her family's gospel choir in St. Louis.
youtube_search: Ann Peebles I Can't Stand the Rain

name: Aretha Franklin
description: In the 1970s, Aretha Franklin continued her reign as the 'Queen of Soul' with a sophisticated blend of gospel-infused soul and R&B. Her work during this decade included landmark live recordings and experimental collaborations that expanded her musical reach. Despite internal struggles at Atlantic Records toward the end of the decade, her vocal power and cultural significance remained unmatched.

key_albums_70s.title: This Girl's in Love with You

key_songs_70s: Until You Come Back to Me (That's What I'm Gonna Do)
trivia: Her 1972 live album 'Amazing Grace' became the best-selling gospel album of all time.
Franklin won eight consecutive Grammy Awards for Best Female R&B Vocal Performance between 1968 and 1975.
In 1972, she released 'Young, Gifted and Black', an album that reflected the social and political consciousness of the era.

name: Barry White
description: Barry White became a major solo star in the 1970s, defined by his deep bass voice and lush, orchestral soul arrangements. His music was a cornerstone of the romantic soul and early disco movements, often featuring spoken-word introductions and a focus on sensuality. As a producer and bandleader for the Love Unlimited Orchestra, he was a dominant force on the pop and R&B charts.

key_albums_70s.title: I've Got So Much to Give
Just Another Way to Say I Love You
Barry White Sings for Someone You Love

key_songs_70s: I'm Gonna Love You Just a Little More Baby
Never, Never Gonna Give Ya Up
Can't Get Enough of Your Love, Babe
You're the First, the Last, My Everything
It's Ecstasy When You Lay Down Next to Me
trivia: White spent four months in prison at age 16 for stealing $30,000 worth of tires, an experience that led him to pursue a music career.
He wrote and produced the #1 hit 'Love's Theme' for the Love Unlimited Orchestra in 1973.
His deep voice and romantic image earned him the nickname 'The Walrus of Love' later in his career.
youtube_search: Barry White Can't Get Enough of Your Love Babe

name: Betty Wright
description: Betty Wright was a powerhouse of 1970s Miami soul, known for her incredible vocal range and whistle register. Her sound incorporated elements of R&B, funk, and early disco, often featuring direct and assertive lyrics. She remained a consistent hitmaker and an influential figure for future generations of R&B singers.

key_albums_70s.title: I Love the Way You Love

trivia: Her signature hit 'Clean Up Woman' reached number six on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1972 and was certified gold.
She won a Grammy Award for Best R&B Song for writing 'Where Is the Love' in 1976.
Wright began her career at age two singing in her family's gospel group, the Echoes of Joy.

name: Bill Withers
description: Bill Withers was a defining voice of the 1970s with a sound that blended soul, folk, and R&B. His honest and relatable songwriting made him a standout artist of the era, focusing on human connection and everyday life. Despite his relatively short recording career, his influence on the genre remains profound and lasting.
key_songs_70s: The Same Love That Made Me Laugh
trivia: He wrote his first hit 'Ain't No Sunshine' while working at a factory making toilet seats for airplanes.
His 1977 hit 'Lovely Day' is famous for the sustained 18-second note he sings toward the end.
Withers largely walked away from the music industry in the mid-1980s, citing conflicts with record company executives.

name: Billy Ocean
description: Billy Ocean emerged in the mid-1970s as a versatile soul and pop artist with British and Caribbean influences. His early sound was a mix of disco-friendly R&B and smooth soul ballads. This period laid the groundwork for his massive international stardom in the following decade.
key_songs_70s: Stop Me (If You've Heard it All Before)
trivia: His 1976 breakthrough single 'Love Really Hurts Without You' reached number two in the UK and number 22 in the US.
Born in Trinidad, he moved to London at age seven and later worked as a tailor in Savile Row.
He was awarded an MBE for services to music in the 2020 New Year Honours.
youtube_search: Billy Ocean Love Really Hurts Without You

name: Billy Paul
description: Billy Paul was a cornerstone of the Philadelphia soul sound during the 1970s, signed to the legendary Philadelphia International Records. His diverse vocal style ranged from mellow and soulful to raspy, and his music often included socially conscious themes. He achieved global fame during this decade with some of the era's most iconic soul recordings.
key_songs_70s: Am I Black Enough for You?
trivia: His 1972 signature hit 'Me and Mrs. Jones' was a number-one smash and won a Grammy Award for Best Male R&B Vocal Performance.
The follow-up single 'Am I Black Enough for You?' was considered controversial at the time and hindered his crossover success.
Questlove has described him as one of the 'criminally unmentioned proprietors' of socially conscious civil rights music.
youtube_search: Billy Paul Me and Mrs. Jones

name: Bobby Womack
description: Bobby Womack was a prolific singer-songwriter and guitarist who flourished in the 1970s with a gritty and emotional brand of soul. His sound blended gospel, blues, and R&B, and he was highly regarded for his distinctive guitar playing as well as his vocals. During this era, he established himself as a 'soul survivor' with a series of influential hits and albums.
key_songs_70s: That's the Way I Feel About Cha
trivia: Womack wrote and originally recorded 'It's All Over Now', which became the Rolling Stones' first UK number one hit.
He began his career as a protege of Sam Cooke and sang lead for his family group, the Valentinos.
In the early 1970s, he recorded 'Across 110th Street', the iconic title track for the film of the same name.

name: Candi Staton
description: Candi Staton was a major soul and disco artist in the 1970s, transitioning from gritty Southern soul to high-energy dance music. Her versatile voice and emotional delivery allowed her to navigate multiple genres successfully. She remains celebrated for her ability to infuse pop and dance tracks with genuine soulful depth.
trivia: Her 1976 disco anthem 'Young Hearts Run Free' was a massive international hit and reached number one on the US R&B charts.
She was married to fellow soul singer Clarence Carter in the early 1970s.
Staton began her career in gospel, touring with the Jewel Gospel Trio as a teenager.
youtube_search: Candi Staton Young Hearts Run Free

name: Chaka Khan
description: Known as the 'Queen of Funk', Chaka Khan rose to prominence in the early 1970s as the lead vocalist for the band Rufus. Her sound was a high-energy mix of funk, soul, and R&B, driven by her extraordinary vocal range and charisma. By the late 1970s, she established a highly successful solo career, further cementing her status as a soul icon.
key_songs_70s: Do You Love What You Feel
trivia: Her 1978 debut solo single 'I'm Every Woman' reached number one on the R&B charts.
Khan was a member of the Black Panther Party in her teens before focusing on music.
She was given the name 'Chaka' by a Yoruba priest when she was 13 years old.

name: Clarence Carter
description: Clarence Carter was a key figure in 1970s Southern soul, known for his deep, booming voice and storytelling ability. His sound was rooted in R&B and blues, often featuring humorous or poignant lyrics about domestic life. He remained a steady presence on the soul charts during the early part of the decade.
trivia: His 1970 hit 'Patches' won a Grammy Award for Best Rhythm & Blues Song.
Carter is blind and earned a music degree from Alabama State University in 1960.
He was once married to soul singer Candi Staton during the peak of his career.

name: Commodores
description: The Commodores were a powerhouse of 1970s funk and soul, rising from their roots in Alabama to become one of Motown's biggest acts. Their sound successfully evolved from gritty, horn-heavy funk to polished soul ballads fronted by Lionel Richie. This versatility allowed them to dominate both the R&B and pop charts throughout the mid-to-late 1970s.
trivia: The group originally met while they were all students at Tuskegee Institute in Alabama.
Their 1978 ballad 'Three Times a Lady' was a global number-one smash and remains one of Motown's biggest hits.
Before their breakthrough, they spent two years as the opening act for the Jackson 5 on tour.

name: Curtis Mayfield
description: Curtis Mayfield was a revolutionary force in 1970s soul, pioneering a sound that blended progressive soul, funk, and psychedelic influences. His work during this decade was highly socially conscious, focusing on themes of African-American identity and inner-city struggles. As a producer and solo artist, he defined the 'Gentle Genius' persona with his distinct falsetto and cinematic musical vision.

key_albums_70s.title: There's No Place Like America Today

trivia: His soundtrack for the 1972 film 'Super Fly' is considered a masterpiece of socially conscious funk and soul.
Mayfield was a central figure in the Chicago soul scene and founded his own label, Curtom Records, in 1970.
He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame twice: once as a member of the Impressions and again as a solo artist.

name: David Ruffin
description: David Ruffin rose to fame as the powerful lead voice of The Temptations before launching a solo career in the early 1970s. His gritty, gospel-tinged tenor was one of the most distinctive sounds in Motown history. His 1975 solo hit Walk Away from Love demonstrated that his voice remained a commanding force outside the group context.

key_albums_70s.title: Me 'N Rock 'N Roll Are Here to Stay

trivia: Walk Away from Love reached number nine on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1975, his biggest solo hit.
Ruffin was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame as a member of The Temptations in 1989.
youtube_search: David Ruffin Walk Away from Love

name: Diana Ross
description: In the 1970s, Diana Ross successfully transitioned from being the lead singer of the Supremes to becoming a massive solo superstar. Her sound evolved from Motown soul to sophisticated pop and eventually pioneered the disco movement. Throughout the decade, she was a dominant force on the charts, in film, and as a global cultural icon.
key_songs_70s: Theme from Mahogany (Do You Know Where You're Going To)
trivia: Her debut solo single 'Ain't No Mountain High Enough' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1970.
She received an Academy Award nomination for Best Actress for her portrayal of Billie Holiday in 'Lady Sings the Blues' (1972).
Ross was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2016 for her contributions to music and culture.

name: Dionne Warwick
description: Dionne Warwick was one of the most successful female vocalists of the 1970s, known for her elegant phrasing and sophisticated pop-soul style. Her collaborations with Burt Bacharach and Hal David defined an era of lush orchestral soul in the early part of the decade. She found renewed commercial success in the late 70s with the Arista label under Clive Davis.
key_songs_70s: I'll Never Love This Way Again
trivia: Then Came You, a duet with Spinners, became her first number one pop hit in 1974.
I'll Never Love This Way Again won her the Grammy Award for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance in 1980.

name: Donny Hathaway
description: Donny Hathaway was one of the most influential soul artists of the 1970s, known for his velvet-smooth voice and exceptional skill as a keyboardist and arranger. His sound blended R&B, gospel, and jazz into a cohesive and deeply emotional style. He is particularly remembered for his landmark solo work and his highly successful duets with Roberta Flack.
key_songs_70s: I Love You More Than You'll Ever Know
The Closer I Get to You
trivia: His 1970 song 'This Christmas' has become a modern holiday standard and is one of the most performed Christmas songs by African-American artists.
Hathaway was posthumously honored with a Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award in 2019.
He began singing gospel as a child prodigy and was known by the stage name 'Donny Pitts' during his early years.
youtube_search: Donny Hathaway A Song for You

name: Eddie Kendricks
description: Eddie Kendricks brought his silky falsetto from The Temptations into a successful solo career throughout the 1970s. His 1973 smash Keep On Truckin became one of the defining funk-soul records of the early decade. His smooth vocal style bridged classic Motown soul with the emerging disco and funk sounds of the mid-70s.
key_songs_70s: Girl You Need a Change of Mind
trivia: Keep On Truckin reached number one on both the pop and R&B charts in 1973.
Boogie Down gave Kendricks a second number one pop hit in 1974, cementing his solo stardom.

name: Etta James
description: Etta James brought decades of raw emotional power into the 1970s, recording for Chess Records and later Warner Bros with a voice that straddled blues, soul, and gospel. Her 1970s work showed a grittier, more introspective side compared to her earlier pop hits. She remained a commanding live performer and a touchstone of authentic American soul throughout the decade.
trivia: Deep in the Night, produced by Jerry Wexler and Allen Toussaint in 1978, is widely regarded as one of her finest albums.
James was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1993, recognized as one of the greatest voices in American music.
youtube_search: Etta James I'd Rather Go Blind

name: Four Tops
description: The Four Tops continued their success into the 1970s, moving from their peak Motown era to significant hits at ABC/Dunhill Records. Their sound remained centered on the powerful baritone lead of Levi Stubbs and tight vocal harmonies. This decade saw them branch out into more experimental soul and disco-influenced R&B while maintaining their status as one of the definitive Motown-rooted vocal groups.
key_songs_70s: Ain't No Woman (Like the One I've Got)
trivia: The group performed for over four decades, from 1953 until 1997, without a single change in their original lineup.
Their 1973 hit 'Ain't No Woman (Like the One I've Got)' was a million-seller and reached number four on the Billboard Hot 100.
They were notable at Motown for being fronted by a baritone lead, Levi Stubbs, while most other male groups used tenors.
youtube_search: Four Tops Ain't No Woman (Like the One I've Got)

name: Gene Chandler
description: Gene Chandler was a veteran of the Chicago soul scene whose career experienced a significant commercial revival in the 1970s. Signing with Chi-Sound Records, he scored a string of dance floor hits that blended classic soul sensibility with the emerging disco sound. His polished vocal style and sharp production made him a consistent presence on the R&B charts throughout the decade.
trivia: Get Down reached number one on the R&B charts in 1978, marking a major comeback nearly two decades into his career.
Chandler had originally scored his biggest hit with Duke of Earl in 1962, making his 70s comeback all the more remarkable.

name: Gladys Knight & the Pips
description: Gladys Knight & the Pips reached their commercial and artistic peak in the 1970s, particularly after moving from Motown to Buddah Records in 1973. Their sound during this era featured powerful, gospel-inflected vocals from Knight and sophisticated vocal arrangements from the Pips. They dominated the charts with a mix of driving soul-funk and poignant, orchestral soul ballads.
key_songs_70s: Neither One of Us (Wants to Be the First to Say Goodbye)
I've Got to Use My Imagination
Best Thing That Ever Happened to Me
trivia: Their signature song 'Midnight Train to Georgia' was a number-one smash and won the 1974 Grammy for Best R&B Vocal Performance by a Duo, Group or Chorus.
The group began their career in 1952 when siblings and cousins formed a group at a family birthday party.
Gladys Knight later became known as the 'Empress of Soul' following her decades-long influence and success in the genre.
youtube_search: Gladys Knight Midnight Train to Georgia

name: Grover Washington Jr.
description: Grover Washington Jr. was the defining voice of soul jazz and smooth R&B saxophone in the 1970s, blending jazz improvisation with soul and funk grooves. His Kudu Records albums were enormously influential in shaping the quiet storm radio format. His rich, warm saxophone tone made him one of the best-selling instrumental artists of the entire decade.
trivia: Mister Magic reached number one on the R&B charts in 1975 and became one of the best-selling jazz albums of the decade.
Washington helped pioneer the quiet storm radio format that would dominate R&B airplay throughout the late 1970s and 1980s.

name: Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes
description: Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes were one of the most successful Philadelphia soul groups of the 1970s, defined by the powerful lead vocals of Teddy Pendergrass. Their sound was a sophisticated mix of soulful R&B, gospel emotion, and emerging disco rhythms, all polished by Gamble and Huff's production. They became a definitive voice of the Philadelphia International Records era.
key_songs_70s: If You Don't Know Me by Now
Hope That We Can Be Together Soon
trivia: Despite Harold Melvin's top billing, the group's massive success in the 1970s was largely due to lead singer Teddy Pendergrass.
The group began as a doo-wop group called 'The Charlemagnes' in the middle of the 1950s.
Their 1975 hit 'Wake Up Everybody' became a defining socially conscious soul anthem of the decade.
youtube_search: Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes Wake Up Everybody

name: Ike & Tina Turner
description: Ike and Tina Turner were one of the most electrifying live acts of the early 1970s, combining raw blues-influenced R&B with Tina's explosive stage presence. Their 1971 recording of Proud Mary became their biggest pop crossover hit, introducing them to a massive new mainstream audience. The duo continued to record and tour heavily until their split in 1976.
key_songs_70s: I Want to Take You Higher
trivia: Their live version of Proud Mary reached number four on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1971 and won a Grammy for Best R&B Vocal Performance.
Nutbush City Limits, written by Tina Turner, became one of their signature songs and a staple of rock radio worldwide.

name: Isaac Hayes
description: Isaac Hayes was a dominant force in 1970s soul, known for his deep baritone voice and grandiose, symphonic soul arrangements. His work during this decade pioneered the 'progressive soul' movement and was instrumental in bringing cinematic scores to R&B music. He remains an iconic figure of the era, recognized for his revolutionary approach to album production and orchestration.
trivia: His 1971 'Theme from Shaft' won an Academy Award for Best Original Song, making him the first African American to win a non-acting Oscar.
Hayes was a key creative force at Stax Records before his massive solo success, writing hits for Sam & Dave.
His 1971 album 'Black Moses' featured unique fold-out packaging that could be arranged as a poster of Hayes.

name: James Brown
description: James Brown was a central figure of 20th-century music, and in the 1970s, he pioneered the funk sound that would revolutionize R&B. His sound during this decade was characterized by percussive rhythms, tight horn arrangements, and a focus on 'the one' (the first beat of a measure). Known as the 'Godfather of Soul', his energy and innovation during the 70s established a template for modern dance music and hip-hop.
key_songs_70s: Get Up (I Feel Like Being a) Sex Machine
trivia: His 1973 album 'The Payback' was originally recorded as a soundtrack for a film but was rejected, eventually becoming one of his most successful solo albums.
Brown was credited with helping to prevent a riot in Boston following the 1968 assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. by performing a televised concert.
He was one of the first ten inductees into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1986.
youtube_search: James Brown Get Up (I Feel Like Being a) Sex Machine

name: Jerry Butler
description: Jerry Butler, known as the Iceman for his cool and controlled vocal delivery, was a major force in Philadelphia soul throughout the 1970s. Working with producers Kenny Gamble and Leon Huff, he helped shape the lush orchestral sound that defined the Philadelphia International Records style. His sophisticated ballads made him one of the most consistently charting R&B artists of the decade.
key_songs_70s: What's the Use of Breaking Up
trivia: Butler recorded a series of critically acclaimed albums with producers Gamble and Huff that helped establish the Philadelphia soul sound.
He earned the nickname the Iceman for his smooth, emotionally controlled vocal style that contrasted with more explosive soul singers of the era.
youtube_search: Jerry Butler Only the Strong Survive

name: Johnnie Taylor
description: Johnnie Taylor was a major force in 1970s soul and R&B, known as the 'Philosopher of Soul' for his relatable storytelling. His sound evolved from the gritty Southern soul of Stax Records to a polished, disco-inflected style at Columbia Records. He remained a dominant presence on the charts throughout the decade, culminating in one of the biggest hits of the era.
key_songs_70s: Jody's Got Your Girl and Gone
I Believe in You (You Believe in Me)
Love Is Better in the A.M.
trivia: His 1976 smash 'Disco Lady' was the first single ever to be certified platinum by the RIAA.
Taylor began his career replacing Sam Cooke in the gospel group the Soul Stirrers in 1957.
He recorded many of his biggest 1970s hits for the legendary Stax Records before moving to Columbia Records.

name: Junior Walker
description: Junior Walker was a unique Motown artist, combining soulful vocals with his iconic, driving tenor saxophone playing. In the 1970s, he continued to release gritty and danceable soul-funk, maintaining the distinctive sound that made him a standout during Motown's peak years. His work remained highly influential for its infectious grooves and raw energy.

key_albums_70s.title: Peace and Understanding Is Hard to Find

key_songs_70s: Do You See My Love (For You Growing)
trivia: Walker was known for playing the saxophone on many of his records, a rarity for lead singers of the era.
He and his band, the All Stars, were one of the first acts signed to Motown's 'Soul' subsidiary label.
Later in his career, he famously performed the saxophone solo on Foreigner's 1981 hit 'Urgent'.
youtube_search: Junior Walker Walk in the Night

name: Lou Rawls
description: Lou Rawls experienced a career resurgence in the mid-1970s after signing with Philadelphia International Records. His sound was a sophisticated blend of soul, jazz, and pop, defined by his unmistakable baritone voice and the polished 'Philly Soul' production. This period established him as a premier balladeer and R&B star of the decade.

key_albums_70s.title: When You Hear Lou, You've Heard It All
Let Me Be Good to You
Sit Down and Talk to Me

key_songs_70s: You'll Never Find Another Love Like Mine
See You When I Git There
Let Me Be Good to You
trivia: His 1976 hit 'You'll Never Find Another Love Like Mine' was a million-seller and his biggest crossover success.
Rawls was a childhood friend of Sam Cooke and the two performed together in gospel groups in Chicago.
He won three Grammy Awards during his career and was famously the face of the United Negro College Fund telethons.
youtube_search: Lou Rawls You'll Never Find Another Love Like Mine

name: Major Lance
description: Major Lance was a Chicago soul veteran whose smooth vocal style had made him a star in the 1960s, and he continued recording through the 1970s for a variety of labels. Though his greatest commercial successes predated the decade, his early 70s recordings for Curtom and other labels kept him visible on the R&B circuit. He was particularly popular in the UK Northern Soul scene throughout the decade.
key_songs_70s: Come On Have Yourself a Good Time
trivia: Lance was a key figure in the Northern Soul movement in Britain, where his 1960s recordings remained enormously popular throughout the 1970s.
He recorded for Curtis Mayfield's Curtom Records in the early 1970s, releasing material that aligned with the socially conscious soul of the era.
youtube_search: Major Lance Um Um Um Um Um Um

name: Marvin Gaye
description: Marvin Gaye was a transformational figure in 1970s soul, leading the shift toward the 'album era' with deeply personal and socially conscious statements. His sound during this decade ranged from the trenchant social commentary of 'What's Going On' to the intense sensuality of 'Let's Get It On'. By the late 1970s, his work became increasingly experimental and introspective, redefining the boundaries of Motown and R&B music.
key_songs_70s: Inner City Blues (Make Me Wanna Holler)
trivia: His 1971 album 'What's Going On' is often cited as one of the greatest albums in the history of popular music.
Gaye's 1978 album 'Here, My Dear' was famously recorded to fulfill a divorce settlement agreement with his first wife, Anna Gordy.
He successfully petitioned Motown to allow him to produce his own work, becoming one of the first artists on the label to gain creative control.

name: Mavis Staples
description: Mavis Staples was one of the most powerful voices in gospel-influenced soul, both as lead singer of The Staple Singers and as a solo artist throughout the 1970s. Her deep, commanding contralto carried messages of social justice and spiritual uplift with unmatched conviction. Her Stax and Curtom recordings in the early 70s produced some of the most emotionally resonant soul music of the decade.
key_songs_70s: I Have Learned to Do Without You
trivia: Staples recorded solo material for Stax Records in the early 1970s while continuing to perform with The Staple Singers.
Her voice was described by Bob Dylan as one of the most extraordinary in American music, and he repeatedly sought her collaboration.
youtube_search: Mavis Staples I'll Take You There

name: Michael Jackson
description: In the 1970s, Michael Jackson navigated a successful transition from a child prodigy to a mature solo artist while continuing to lead the Jackson 5. His sound evolved from bubblegum soul to sophisticated R&B and disco, culminating in the landmark 'Off the Wall' at the end of the decade. This period established his incredible vocal technique and global appeal before he became the 'King of Pop'.
trivia: His 1972 single 'Ben' was a number-one hit and won a Golden Globe for Best Original Song.
At age 13, he became the youngest solo artist ever to top the Billboard Hot 100 with 'Ben'.
His 1979 album 'Off the Wall' was the first solo album by an artist to generate four top-ten hits on the Billboard Hot 100.
youtube_search: Michael Jackson Don't Stop 'Til You Get Enough

name: Millie Jackson
description: Millie Jackson was a unique and influential voice in 1970s soul, known for her sexually explicit and humorous spoken-word 'raps' integrated into her songs. Her sound was a mix of gritty Southern soul and polished R&B production. She redefined the 'soul singer' persona with her assertive and often controversial themes of love and heartbreak.
key_songs_70s: If Loving You Is Wrong (I Don't Want to Be Right)
If You're Not Back in Love By Monday
trivia: Her 1974 concept album 'Caught Up' is considered a landmark in soul music for its narrative structure.
Jackson is often referred to as the 'Mother of Hip-Hop' due to her extensive use of spoken-word sections.
Three of her 1970s albums were certified gold by the RIAA for sales over 500,000 copies.
youtube_search: Millie Jackson It Hurts So Good

name: Minnie Riperton
description: Minnie Riperton was a unique talent in 1970s soul, best known for her stunning five-octave vocal range and her use of the whistle register. Her sound blended R&B, soul, and pop with subtle jazz and psychedelic influences. Her life and career were tragically cut short, but her musical legacy as the 'operatic angel' of soul remains influential.
trivia: Her 1975 single 'Lovin' You' was a global number-one smash and is famous for her bird-like high notes.
Before her solo fame, she was the lead singer for the psychedelic soul band Rotary Connection.
She was one of the first public figures to speak openly about her diagnosis and treatment for breast cancer.

name: Natalie Cole
description: Natalie Cole emerged in the mid-1970s as one of the most exciting new voices in soul and R&B, quickly establishing herself as a hitmaker independent of her famous father Nat King Cole. Her debut single This Will Be became an immediate classic, earning her two Grammy Awards in 1976. Throughout the late 70s she delivered a string of polished, energetic R&B albums that made her one of Capitol Records' biggest stars.
key_songs_70s: I've Got Love on My Mind
trivia: This Will Be won Grammy Awards for Best New Artist and Best R&B Vocal Performance at the 1976 ceremony.
She scored five consecutive top ten R&B albums between 1975 and 1979, making her one of the decade's most consistent soul hitmakers.

name: Nina Simone
description: Nina Simone was a singular artist who defied categorization, blending jazz, soul, gospel, blues, and classical music into a deeply personal artistic statement throughout the 1970s. Her 1970s recordings showed an increasingly bold political consciousness alongside extraordinary musical sophistication. Though she relocated to Liberia and later Barbados during the decade, her recordings and live performances remained landmark events in Black American music.
key_songs_70s: To Be Young Gifted and Black
trivia: To Be Young Gifted and Black became an anthem of the Black Power movement and one of the most celebrated protest songs of the era.
Simone's eclectic musical range and uncompromising political stance made her one of the most distinctive and influential artists of her generation.

name: Patti LaBelle
description: After leading the groundbreaking funk-soul trio Labelle, Patti LaBelle launched a highly successful solo career in the late 1970s. Her sound was defined by her immense vocal power and high-octane stage presence, blending soul, R&B, and rock influences. She established herself as a premiere diva of the era, known for her incredible vocal range and emotional intensity.
key_songs_70s: Music Is My Way of Life
trivia: As a member of Labelle, she scored the massive 1974 number-one hit 'Lady Marmalade'.
Her 1977 debut solo album featured the classic R&B ballad 'You Are My Friend'.
She is known as the 'Godmother of Soul' for her decades-long influence and success in the genre.
youtube_search: Patti LaBelle You Are My Friend

name: Peabo Bryson
description: Peabo Bryson emerged in the late 1970s as one of the smoothest and most romantic voices in R&B, becoming a fixture on the quiet storm radio format with his velvet baritone. He signed with Capitol Records and released a string of polished soul albums that established him as a premier balladeer. His gift for sophisticated love songs made him one of the most sought-after voices in adult contemporary R&B.
trivia: Reaching for the Sky reached number one on the R&B charts in 1977, establishing Bryson as a major force in the quiet storm soul movement.
He became known for his romantic duets, a style he developed through his late 70s recordings that would define his career for decades.
youtube_search: Peabo Bryson Reaching for the Sky

name: Phyllis Hyman
description: Phyllis Hyman emerged in the late 1970s as a sophisticated voice in the soul and jazz scenes. Her sound was a foundational part of the 'quiet storm' genre, characterized by romantic ballads and smooth, orchestral production. She was highly regarded for her elegant vocal style and became a favorite of soul music aficionados during this period.

key_albums_70s.title: You Know How to Love Me

key_songs_70s: You Know How to Love Me
trivia: Her 1979 hit 'You Know How to Love Me' became a definitive late-70s dance-soul classic.
Before her recording career, she worked as a successful fashion model in New York.
She was a classically trained singer and attended the Pittsburgh Music Strategy program.
youtube_search: Phyllis Hyman You Know How to Love Me

name: Rufus
description: Rufus was a definitive funk and soul band of the 1970s, fronted by the extraordinary vocals of Chaka Khan. Their sound was characterized by tight, syncopated rhythms, sophisticated musicianship, and a seamless blend of R&B and rock. Throughout the decade, they remained a top-tier act on both the charts and the live circuit, defining the funk-soul aesthetic of the era.
key_songs_70s: At Midnight (My Love Will Lift You Up)
Do You Love What You Feel
trivia: Their hit 'Tell Me Something Good' was written for them by Stevie Wonder and became a signature track of the funk era.
The band was originally created from two Philadelphia groups but relocated to Chicago where they achieved stardom.
The group earned a total of two Grammy Awards during their peak years in the 1970s.

name: Smokey Robinson
description: After retiring from the Miracles in 1972, Smokey Robinson launched a highly successful solo career in the mid-1970s. His sound during this era was defined by elegant, sophisticated soul and he became a pioneer of the 'quiet storm' radio format. As Motown's vice president and a premier songwriter, he remained a central figure in the evolution of soulful pop throughout the decade.
trivia: His 1975 album 'A Quiet Storm' gave its name to a popular and enduring format of soul music radio.
Robinson was the first solo artist to receive a Hollywood Walk of Fame star as both a solo act and as a group member.
He wrote many of Motown's biggest hits for other artists, including the Temptations and Mary Wells.

name: Stevie Wonder
description: The 1970s are considered Stevie Wonder's 'classic period,' during which he released a series of groundbreaking and highly influential albums. His sound during this era was defined by his masterful use of synthesizers and multi-instrumental skill, creating cohesive and socially conscious artistic statements. He successfully broke away from the traditional Motown production model to achieve complete creative freedom and critical acclaim.

key_albums_70s.title: Songs in the Key of Life
Journey Through 'The Secret Life of Plants'

key_songs_70s: You Are the Sunshine of My Life
trivia: Wonder won the Grammy for Album of the Year for three consecutive album releases in the 1970s.
He was a child prodigy known as 'Little Stevie Wonder' and had his first number-one hit at age 13.
His 1976 double album 'Songs in the Key of Life' was the first by an American artist to debut at number one on the Billboard charts.

name: Teddy Pendergrass
description: After achieving fame as the lead singer of Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes, Teddy Pendergrass launched a massive solo career in the late 1970s. His sound was the quintessence of romantic Philadelphia soul, characterized by his powerful baritone and masculine vulnerability. He became one of the biggest sex symbols and R&B stars of the decade, known for his sold-out 'women only' concerts.

key_albums_70s.title: Life Is a Song Worth Singing

key_songs_70s: The Love I Lost (with Blue Notes)
trivia: His first four solo albums were all certified platinum, a record-breaking feat for an R&B artist at the time.
Pendergrass began his musical career as a drummer for the Blue Notes before Harold Melvin moved him to lead vocals.
He was the first soul artist to hold highly successful 'women-only' midnight concerts.

name: The Chi-Lites
description: The Chi-Lites were a leading vocal group of the 1970s Chicago soul scene, known for their lush harmonies and romantic ballads. Their sound, often written and produced by lead singer Eugene Record, combined smooth vocal arrangements with orchestral production. They achieved significant crossover success on both the R&B and pop charts during the early to mid-1970s.

key_albums_70s.title: (For God's Sake) Give More Power to the People

key_songs_70s: (For God's Sake) Give More Power to the People
The Coldest Days of My Life
trivia: Their 1972 hit 'Oh Girl' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100.
The group formed at Chicago's Hyde Park High School in 1959.
Lead singer Eugene Record was also a successful songwriter and producer for the group and other artists.

name: The Delfonics
description: The Delfonics were pioneers of the 1970s Philadelphia soul sound, known for their smooth vocal harmonies and high-pitched falsetto leads. Their sound was defined by the sophisticated production of Thom Bell, featuring lush orchestration and romantic themes. They were one of the most successful vocal groups of the early 1970s, setting a template for the Philly soul aesthetic.

key_albums_70s.title: Tell Me This Is a Dream

key_songs_70s: Didn't I (Blow Your Mind This Time)
When You Get Right Down to It
Tell Me This Is a Dream
trivia: Their 1970 classic 'Didn't I (Blow Your Mind This Time)' won a Grammy Award for Best R&B Performance by a Duo or Group.
The group's sound was a direct precursor to the success of the Philadelphia International Records label.
Lead singer William Hart's distinct falsetto was a hallmark of the group's sound.
youtube_search: The Delfonics Didn't I (Blow Your Mind This Time)

name: The Dells
description: The Dells were one of the most enduring vocal groups in soul music, reinventing themselves in the late 1960s with producer Charles Stepney and carrying that success deep into the 1970s. Their rich blend of bass and falsetto voices produced some of the most emotionally complex soul records of the era. Their Cadet Records recordings set a standard for sophisticated group soul that influenced countless acts throughout the decade.

key_albums_70s.title: Give Your Baby a Standing Ovation

key_songs_70s: Give Your Baby a Standing Ovation
Learning to Love You Was Easy
We Got to Get Our Thing Together
trivia: Give Your Baby a Standing Ovation reached number four on the R&B charts in 1973, one of their biggest hits of the decade.
The Dells remained together with almost no lineup changes for over four decades, one of the most stable group configurations in soul music history.
youtube_search: The Dells Give Your Baby a Standing Ovation

name: The Emotions
description: The Emotions were a premier female vocal group of the 1970s, known for their intricate harmonies and upbeat soul sound. After starting at Stax Records, they achieved massive success in the late 1970s after collaborating with Maurice White of Earth, Wind & Fire. Their sound during this peak period blended soul, disco, and R&B into a series of massive international hits.
key_songs_70s: I Don't Wanna Lose Your Love
trivia: Their 1977 smash 'Best of My Love' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 and won a Grammy Award.
The group began their career as a gospel group called 'The Hutchinson Sunbeams' in the late 1950s.
They collaborated with Earth, Wind & Fire on the 1979 classic 'Boogie Wonderland'.
youtube_search: The Emotions Best of My Love

name: The Isley Brothers
description: The Isley Brothers reinvented themselves in the 1970s as a high-energy funk-rock and soul unit, expanding to a sextet known as '3 + 3'. Their sound was a powerful blend of soulful vocals, driving funk rhythms, and searing rock guitar work. This era was the group's most successful, with a series of multi-platinum albums that redefined their legacy for a new generation.
trivia: The group's move to a self-contained band in 1973 led to their greatest commercial peak with the album '3 + 3'.
Their 1975 anthem 'Fight the Power' reached number one on the R&B charts and reflected the social tensions of the decade.
They were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1992.

name: The Jackson 5
description: The Jackson 5 were a global phenomenon in the early 1970s, defining the 'bubblegum soul' sound for Motown Records. Their music was characterized by high-energy R&B, tight vocal harmonies, and the extraordinary lead vocals of a young Michael Jackson. By the mid-1970s, they transitioned to CBS Records as 'The Jacksons', evolving into a more mature funk and disco-oriented group.
key_songs_70s: Shake Your Body (Down to the Ground)
trivia: The group was discovered by Motown artist Bobby Taylor in 1968 and became the first act to have their first four singles reach number one.
They popularized the 'Robot' dance move with their 1974 hit 'Dancing Machine'.
In 1976, they signed with Epic Records and were renamed 'The Jacksons' due to a legal dispute with Motown.

name: The Manhattans
description: The Manhattans achieved their greatest fame in the 1970s as masters of the smooth soul ballad. Their sound was characterized by polished vocal harmonies and emotional storytelling, often featuring spoken-word sections by Winfred 'Blue' Lovett. They remained consistent hitmakers on the R&B charts, delivering one of the decade's biggest international smashes.

key_albums_70s.title: That's How Much I Love You

trivia: Their 1976 signature hit 'Kiss and Say Goodbye' was only the second single in history to be certified platinum by the RIAA.
Lead singer Gerald Alston, who joined in 1970, was the nephew of Shirley Alston Reeves of the Shirelles.
The group was inducted into the Atlantic City Walk of Fame in September 2024.
youtube_search: The Manhattans Kiss and Say Goodbye

name: The Miracles
description: After Smokey Robinson's departure in 1972, The Miracles successfully reinvented themselves with new lead singer Billy Griffin. Their 1970s sound transitioned from classic Motown soul to upbeat, disco-influenced R&B. They remained a significant force on the charts, achieving one of the biggest dance hits of the mid-1970s.
key_songs_70s: I Don't Blame You at All
trivia: Their 1975 hit 'Love Machine' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 and became a multi-million seller.
The group was Motown's first successful vocal group and were once known as 'The Matadors'.
They were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2012 as a group after Smokey Robinson had been inducted as a solo artist in 1987.

name: The O'Jays
description: The O'Jays were at the forefront of the Philadelphia soul movement in the 1970s, recording for Philadelphia International Records. Their sound featured high-energy vocal performances and sophisticated production by Gamble & Huff, often incorporating socially conscious and political themes. They became one of the most successful and respected vocal groups of the era, dominating both the R&B and pop charts.
key_songs_70s: Give the People What They Want
trivia: Their 1973 hit 'Love Train' reached number one on both the Billboard R&B and Pop charts.
The group originally formed in 1958 and went through several name changes, including 'The Triumphs' and 'The Mascots'.
They were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2005.

name: The Spinners
description: The Spinners achieved massive commercial success in the 1970s after moving to Atlantic Records and collaborating with producer Thom Bell. Their sound became a definitive example of polished Philadelphia soul, characterized by tight vocal harmonies and smooth arrangements. Throughout the decade, they remained a dominant force on the charts with a series of major international hits.

key_albums_70s.title: Happiness Is Being With the Spinners

key_songs_70s: Could It Be I'm Falling in Love
trivia: In the UK, they were often billed as the 'Detroit Spinners' to avoid confusion with a British folk group.
The group received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame on June 30, 1976.
They were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2023.

name: The Staple Singers
description: The Staple Singers achieved their greatest commercial peak in the 1970s by blending their gospel roots with soul and funk production at Stax Records. Their sound during this era was defined by powerful, socially conscious lyrics and Mavis Staples' distinctive lead vocals. They became a definitive voice of the era, successfully bridging the gap between spiritual and secular music.
key_songs_70s: If You're Ready (Come Go with Me)
Touch a Hand, Make a Friend
trivia: Their 1972 hit 'I'll Take You There' reached number one on both the Billboard R&B and Pop charts.
The group originally formed as a gospel unit in 1948, with 'Pops' Staples performing with his children.
They were featured prominently in the 1972 documentary film 'Wattstax' and the accompanying live album.
youtube_search: The Staple Singers I'll Take You There

name: The Stylistics
description: The Stylistics were a definitive Philadelphia soul group of the 1970s, known for their smooth vocal harmonies and Russell Thompkins Jr.'s distinctive falsetto lead. Their sound was characterized by lush, romantic ballads and the sophisticated production of Thom Bell and Linda Creed. During the early to mid-1970s, they achieved a string of twelve consecutive R&B top ten hits, making them one of the most successful vocal groups of the era.
key_songs_70s: I'm Stone in Love with You
You Make Me Feel Brand New
trivia: The group had five gold singles and three gold albums certified by the RIAA.
In 1975, 'The Best of The Stylistics' was the best-selling album of the year in the United Kingdom.
They were inducted into the Vocal Group Hall of Fame in 2004.
youtube_search: The Stylistics You Make Me Feel Brand New

name: The Temptations
description: In the 1970s, The Temptations transitioned from their psychedelic soul period to a harder-edged funk sound while remaining a dominant force at Motown. Their music during this era featured complex vocal arrangements and socially relevant lyrics produced by Norman Whitfield. Despite several lineup changes, they continued to influence the genre with their innovative sound and precise choreography.
key_songs_70s: Just My Imagination (Running Away with Me)
Superstar (Remember How You Got Where You Are)
trivia: Their 1972 classic 'Papa Was a Rollin' Stone' won three Grammy Awards and was a number-one hit on the Billboard Hot 100.
The group was originally formed as 'The Elgins' before signing with Motown.
In 2013, they were honored with the Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award.
youtube_search: The Temptations Papa Was a Rollin' Stone

name: Tina Turner
description: Following her split from Ike Turner in 1976, Tina Turner embarked on a solo career that began modestly but showcased the full raw power of one of rock and soul's greatest live performers. Her late 1970s solo recordings demonstrated her ability to hold her own across soul, rock, and disco genres. Though her massive commercial comeback would come in the 1980s, her groundwork in the late 70s established her independent identity as an artist.
key_songs_70s: Root Toot Undisputable Rock and Roller
trivia: Turner left Ike Turner in July 1976 with only 36 cents and a Mobil credit card, beginning her solo career from scratch.
Her late 70s solo albums were produced in a rock and disco crossover style that foreshadowed her massive 1980s comeback.

name: Tyrone Davis
description: Tyrone Davis was a significant figure in 1970s Chicago soul, known for his smooth, slightly husky vocal delivery. His sound blended bluesy soul with polished R&B production, making him a consistent favorite on the soul charts. Throughout the decade, he established himself as a premier balladeer with a series of influential and relatable hits.

key_albums_70s.title: Turn Back the Hands of Time
I Had It All the Time
In the Mood with Tyrone Davis

key_songs_70s: Turn Back the Hands of Time
I Had It All the Time
trivia: His 1970 single 'Turn Back the Hands of Time' reached number three on the Billboard Hot 100.
Davis worked as a driver for fellow soul singer Freddie King before his own career took off.
He was known by the nickname 'TD' among his fans and fellow musicians.
youtube_search: Tyrone Davis Turn Back the Hands of Time

name: William Bell
description: William Bell was a foundational figure in the Memphis soul sound, having written and recorded for Stax Records since its earliest days. His warm, understated vocal style and gift for emotional songwriting made him one of the most respected artists in Southern soul throughout the 1970s. His Mercury Records recordings in the mid-70s continued the classic Memphis soul tradition with contemporary production.
trivia: Trying to Love Two reached number one on the R&B charts in 1977, his biggest chart success as a solo artist.
Bell co-wrote the Stax classic You Don't Miss Your Water, one of the most influential songs in the Southern soul canon.
youtube_search: William Bell Trying to Love Two

name: Wilson Pickett
description: The 'Wicked Pickett' continued to release powerful soul and funk records into the early 1970s, notably working with Gamble & Huff in Philadelphia. His sound during this era remained raw and energetic, characterized by his gravelly vocals and driving rhythms. While his greatest commercial peak was in the 1960s, his 1970s output solidified his reputation as a definitive soul shouter.
key_songs_70s: Don't Let the Green Grass Fool You
trivia: His 1971 single 'Don't Let the Green Grass Fool You' was a million-seller and certified gold by the RIAA.
Pickett began his career singing in the gospel group The Violinares before joining the Falcons.
He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1991.
